nativeRange.habitat: Myers' Hillstream Loach inhabits rapid-flowing, well-oxygenated mountain streams with boulder and gravel substrates. In the wild it spends most of its time adhered to rock surfaces, grazing the thin layer of algae, diatoms, and biofilm — collectively called aufwuchs — that coats submerged stones in fast-moving hillstream environments.

decorPreferences: water-worn boulders and rounded river rocks for grazing surfaces
strong unidirectional flow (10×+ turnover per hour)
Anubias and Microsorum anchored to rockwork
bright lighting to promote biofilm and algae growth

diet.notes: Myers' Hillstream Loach is a dedicated grazer that spends most of its waking hours scraping periphytic algae, diatoms, filamentous cyanobacteria, and biofilm from rock surfaces. In captivity, algae-covered stones should be present at all times, as fish kept without adequate biofilm tend to decline. Supplement with algae wafers, spirulina-based sinking pellets, and blanched vegetables such as spinach, kale, or zucchini; occasional live or frozen foods — Daphnia, Artemia, or bloodworm — are also accepted and help vary the diet.

goodWith: other hillstream loaches of different genera
small plecos adapted to cooler, high-flow conditions
avoidWith: other Pseudogastromyzon (territorial with conspecifics in small tanks; keep in larger groups or separate)
slow-moving or delicate fish intolerant of strong flow

summary: Myers’ Hillstream Loach (Pseudogastromyzon myersi) is a small, torpedo-shaped algae grazer from fast-flowing streams in Guangdong Province and Hong Kong. Adults reach 4.5 to 6 cm. The species needs cool, highly oxygenated water with strong current and tends to thrive when kept in groups of six or more.
careNotes: Myers’ Hillstream Loach (Pseudogastromyzon myersi) is native to the fast-flowing streams of Guangdong Province and Hong Kong, where it spends its life adhered to boulders and cobbles in highly oxygenated, cool water. Replicating this environment is the central challenge: water temperature should be kept between 18 and 24°C (a chiller or basement installation is often required in warm climates), turnover should exceed 10× the tank volume per hour, and dissolved oxygen should remain very high. A long-footprint tank (minimum 75 cm) with much of the floor space covered by smooth river stones and boulders is strongly preferred over a deep or heavily planted setup. Bright lighting is beneficial because it drives biofilm and algae growth on rock surfaces — this aufwuchs (the thin layer of algae and microorganisms coating submerged stones) is the species’ primary food source and should always be present. Supplement with algae wafers and sinking spirulina-based foods daily; occasional frozen Daphnia or Artemia can also be offered. Males tend to be territorial with conspecifics and similarly-shaped loaches when space is limited; keeping a group of six or more in an appropriately sized tank generally distributes this aggression. The species is an adept climber, so a tightly fitting lid with no gaps is essential. Water quality should be kept very clean — perform regular partial water changes and aim to keep nitrate below around 10–15 ppm. Soft to moderately hard, slightly acidic to neutral water (pH 6.0–7.5, gH 2–12) matches the low-mineral hillstream habitat. This species is not well suited to typical tropical community setups because its cool temperature requirement is incompatible with most warm-water fish.
breedingNotes: Myers' Hillstream Loach has been bred in captivity by specialist keepers, though success is uncommon. Males dig shallow depressions in fine gravel and court females with fin displays; after spawning, the eggs are covered. Incubation lasts approximately 4 to 14 days depending on water temperature. Fry are very small at hatching and need infusoria or green water as their first food, gradually transitioning to micro-algae and finely powdered sinking foods. No parental care is provided once spawning is complete. Conditioning the adults beforehand with a varied diet that includes live foods tends to improve the likelihood of spawning.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Pseudogastromyzon myersi
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (50–60 mm), temperature 18.9–23.9°C, pH 6.0–7.5, hardness 1–12°H, native range (Hong Kong and Guangdong), substrate recommendations, flow requirements (>10×/hr turnover), diet (biofilm specialist), grouping (6+), and breeding notes (gravel-nest spawning, 4–14 days incubation).

additional.name: FishBase — Pseudogastromyzon myersi
additional.notes: Cross-checked max size (6 cm TL), native range (Guangdong Province, China; Hong Kong), taxonomy (Gastromyzontidae, Cypriniformes), habitat (rapid-flowing freshwater streams), diet (periphytic algae and detritus on rock surfaces). Common length 2.9 cm SL noted; 6 cm TL used as adult maximum per species brief.

additional.name: Loaches Online — Pseudogastromyzon myersi
additional.notes: Specialist loach resource. Confirmed temperature 20–23.8°C, pH 7.0–8.0, hardness max 12 dH, adult size 6 cm, high-flow requirement, diet (flake, wafers, bloodworm, algae), and spawning behaviour (male digs hole, fin display, covers eggs). Synonym Hemimyzon myersi noted.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Hillstream Loach Care Guide
additional.notes: General hillstream loach trade care guide. Confirmed temperature range 20–27°C for the group, diet (algae wafers, gel foods, frozen spirulina brine shrimp, blanched vegetables), tight-fitting lid requirement, and compatibility with peaceful community fish. Note: guide focuses primarily on Sewellia lineolata; parameters cross-checked against SeriouslyFish for P. myersi specifics.

additional.name: GBIF — Pseudogastromyzon myersi Herre, 1932
additional.notes: Confirmed taxonomic authority (Herre, 1932), taxonomy (Gastromyzontidae, Cypriniformes), and geographic occurrence data (Guangdong Province, China; Hong Kong). Used to verify classification.

fish.breedingNotes: Males dig gravel spawning depressions and court females through fin displays; eggs are covered after fertilisation. Incubation lasts approximately 4 to 14 days depending on water temperature. Fry require infusoria and micro-algae initially before transitioning to finely powdered foods. Achieving spawning generally requires specialist-level conditioning: pristine water quality, high flow, and a varied diet including live foods.
